description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: Explain how the design and material considerations of a cryogenic electromagnetic linear stage enable it to achieve frictionless movement and high positioning accuracy in cryogenic environments, and discuss how these features address the limitations of traditional piezo-driven stick-slip actuators in wafer probing applications.

gold_context_sources.section_title: Electromagnetic Linear Stage for cryogenic environment
gold_context_sources.evidence: The invention concerns an electromagnetically driven linear stage, specially designed for use in a cryogenic environment. Due to this approach, a fully contactless drive concept without any friction can be realized to create movement. Special attention has been made to cope with the differences in thermal expansion while cooling down to a few Kelvin. Also, dissipation has been minimized by keeping the electromagnetic coils in a temperature range of low resistance by cooling them and monitoring their resistance and temperature. An actively controlled element, which creates a friction-based locking between the moving table and the base frame, has been introduced as an option to cancel out any dissipation in the coils when the desired end position is reached. ... Present commercially available positioning systems in cryogenic environments are based on slip-stick drives with piezo actuators, These positioning systems have a few drawbacks: 1) Since their concept is based on alternatively stick and slip, wear will occur in the slippage phase which will lead to limited lifetime and lack of reliability. 2) The speed of piezo driven stick-slip actuators is practically limited to about 1 mm/s which leads to long repositioning times of about 10 seconds for a typical 10 mm stroke. These drawbacks can be mitigated by a completely different drive concept, based on electromagnetic forces, which is fully contactless without any friction involved to create movement. This electromagnetic linear motor has been combined with a guide concept to enable movements in one direction with special attention to make this electromagnetic linear stage cryogenic and vacuum compatible.

ground_truth_answer: The cryogenic electromagnetic linear stage achieves frictionless movement and high positioning accuracy through a fully contactless electromagnetic drive concept that eliminates friction by avoiding mechanical contact during motion. This is critical in cryogenic environments where traditional mechanical systems face challenges such as wear and limited lifetime due to stick-slip mechanisms. The design incorporates elastic elements like leaf springs to accommodate differential thermal expansion at temperatures down to a few Kelvin, preventing mechanical stresses that could degrade performance. Additionally, the electromagnetic coils are actively cooled and monitored to maintain low electrical resistance, minimizing power dissipation and ensuring stable operation. An optional friction-based locking mechanism is included to lock the moving table once the desired position is reached, thus eliminating coil dissipation during static operation. These features collectively address the drawbacks of piezo-driven stick-slip actuators, which suffer from wear due to their stick-slip nature and have limited speed (~1 mm/s), resulting in long repositioning times (about 10 seconds for 10 mm strokes). By contrast, the electromagnetic linear stage offers contactless motion, higher speed, improved reliability, and compatibility with cryogenic and vacuum environments, making it particularly suited for high-accuracy sequential wafer probing of hundreds of chips on large wafers.
